display_name: Angel Island Immigration Station — Arrival and Detention

segments.from: San Francisco Harbor (arriving vessel at anchor)
segments.to: Angel Island Immigration Station, San Francisco Bay
segments.distance: approximately 5 miles by ferry from the San Francisco waterfront
segments.travel_time: 30 minutes by ferry; arrival processing could begin the same day or days later depending on backlog
segments.cost: no additional fare — transportation to Angel Island was compulsory for detained immigrants
segments.conditions: Chinese passengers were separated from other immigrants aboard the arriving ship and transferred by ferry to Angel Island in San Francisco Bay. The station sat on a rocky hillside overlooking Raccoon Strait, surrounded by eucalyptus trees and fog. New arrivals were stripped, examined for parasites and trachoma, and assigned to segregated barracks — men in one building, women and children in another. The barracks were two-story wooden structures with rows of triple-tiered metal bunks. Detainees were locked inside except during meals and brief exercise periods in a fenced yard.

segments.to: San Francisco, California (release to the Chinese community or deportation)
segments.distance: 5 miles return to San Francisco; or deportation back across the Pacific
segments.travel_time: Detention lasted from 2 weeks to 2 years. The average stay was 2-3 weeks, but complex cases — especially paper son claims — dragged on for months while inspectors cross-checked testimony against coaching books and prior interrogation records.
segments.cost: no monetary cost for detention; the cost was measured in time, uncertainty, and despair. Approximately 10-15% of Chinese detainees were ultimately deported.
segments.conditions: The Board of Special Inquiry interrogated each detainee for hours, asking minute questions about home village geography — how many steps to the well, which direction the front door faced, how many windows in the neighbor's house — designed to catch inconsistencies between the applicant's testimony and that of witnesses in San Francisco. Detainees carved poetry into the wooden barracks walls in classical Chinese, expressing homesickness, rage, and resignation. One poem reads: 'Imprisoned in this wooden building day after day, my freedom withheld — how can I bear to talk about it?' Over the station's thirty-year operation, an estimated 175,000 Chinese immigrants passed through Angel Island, along with Japanese, Korean, South Asian, and other Pacific immigrants.

who_used_it: Primarily Chinese immigrants subject to the Chinese Exclusion Act (1882-1943), including paper sons claiming fraudulent American citizenship, merchants presenting business credentials, and wives of American-born Chinese. Also processed Japanese picture brides, Korean political refugees, South Asian laborers, and other Pacific-origin immigrants. The station processed approximately 500,000 immigrants total between 1910 and 1940, with Chinese detainees held the longest on average.
narrative_hook: In {year}, {ancestor_name} arrived at {destination} not through any golden gate but through the locked barracks of Angel Island, where immigration inspectors asked how many steps led to the front door of a house in {origin} — and a wrong answer meant deportation across 8,000 miles of ocean.
source_references: Him Mark Lai, Genny Lim, and Judy Yung, 'Island: Poetry and History of Chinese Immigrants on Angel Island, 1910-1940' (University of Washington Press, 1991)
Erika Lee and Judy Yung, 'Angel Island: Immigrant Gateway to America' (Oxford University Press, 2010)
Roger Daniels, 'Coming to America: A History of Immigration and Ethnicity in American Life' (HarperPerennial, 2002)
Robert Barde, 'Immigration at the Golden Gate: Passenger Ships, Exclusion, and Angel Island' (Praeger, 2008)
